item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello! Thank you for the comment!

Show, don't tell. Throwing loads of explicit information isn't engaging; the revelation would have been more powerful if the reader could deduce it from the actions and behaviour of the fungus.

Incidentally, we were told frequently during the review process to trim down a fair bit of the content, and to make the information obtained more straightforward. We'll see if we can find a balance between that and what you've suggested!

With regards to the changes suggested:

+ show block
– hide block

This is obvious; they were added after the Foundation had investigated the object.

That statement was added to indicate that the fungus didn't find the D-Class "interesting" enough to make replicas of. Do you have a suggestion of a rewording to make that point clearer?

For example — stating that SCP-4984 hasn't reacted this way to any other creature than the Foundation agents investigating it

Wouldn't this be "telling"?

and that these replicas are the only structures found to throw such blasts of spores towards specific targets.

The article indicates (as far as I remember anyway, there were a lot of revisions on this because we got some pretty divisive feedback) that fungus uses spores as a primary spreading mechanism. These structures aren't the only ones to blast spores at specific targets.

I don't understand. Do you mean that, despite being a two-dimensional figure, it has three-dimensional volume? The phrasing seems wrong.

This is a vestigial error—originally the anomaly was a 3-D representation of a Penrose triangle. Thanks for pointing that out, I've edited it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting concept; I enjoyed the descriptions of places created by the fungus, and the trapped creatures add a good touch of subtle horror. However, I think the article tanks at the end when it starts explaining huge twists about the object without showing us the thrill of how they were discovered.

Show, don't tell. Throwing loads of explicit information isn't engaging; the revelation would have been more powerful if the reader could deduce it from the actions and behaviour of the fungus. At the moment, I'll have to -1.

I don't think this will do particularly bad in terms of rating — but, for the reason stated, I don't think it's exciting enough to do particularly well. If you want to, I'd encourage you to make a few changes and address this issue.

+ I propose to change a few sentences
- hide

The most recent structures to be added in this manner are highly realistic representations of two MTF agents, the first non-D-Class Foundation personnel sent to investigate the instance (it is noted that the D-Class who investigated instance SCP-4984-H did not report any familiar human formations)

This is obvious; they were added after the Foundation had investigated the object. Don't state the obvious — I suggest cutting out the blue sentence.

When approached closely by one of the agents, the structures emitted large clouds of spores, temporarily halting the investigation. It is unknown whether this incident was due to a natural defence mechanism or SCP-4984 purposely drawing the MTF agent closer using their likeness as a lure.

Don't tell; show. Telling us that the fungus is trying to harm Foundation agents isn't exciting. Instead, show clues that allow the readers to guess it themselves and let them thrill at the realization. For example — stating that SCP-4984 hasn't reacted this way to any other creature than the Foundation agents investigating it, and that these replicas are the only structures found to throw such blasts of spores towards specific targets.

The triangle measures 4 m3 and is intact on all angles, seemingly breaking non-anomalous physics.

I don't understand. Do you mean that, despite being a two-dimensional figure, it has three-dimensional volume? The phrasing seems wrong.

If you want to show instead of telling, just remove the note at the end and add more logs that show clues of the SCP's sapience and hostile attitude towards the Foundation. Whatever you choose to do, good luck!

item.title: Re: Credits and external links
item.content:encoded: Big thanks to everyone in chat who took a look at this! It's been through many revisions, with lots of different suggestions for edits, over the course of 4 months or so. Trying to co-author over different timezones can be pretty tricky!

item.title: Credits and external links
item.content:encoded:
Co-authors: DrIsmael, Zyn

Image Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pleurocybella_porrigens.JPG

Forum Reviewers: revlark

Chat Reviewers: DrLeibowitz, bloohued, DB, geckoguy, TRutherford, RockTeethMothEyes, Gishface, MhateT, Rounderhouse, tawny, Rimple, calamitous1, riyangendut
